chunk_summary: Guidelines for hydration assessment and oral rehydration in children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), emphasizing standard ORS use, observation thresholds, and emergency stabilization protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the hydration assessment criteria for children with MAM.
Differentiate between the use of low-osmolarity ORS and ReSoMal based on malnutrition severity.
Apply appropriate oral rehydration plans for children with MAM and dehydration.
Recognize when to escalate care for children with severe dehydration or shock.
key_facts: Children with MAM and dehydration but not shock should receive low-osmolarity ORS per standard diarrhea guidelines.
ReSoMal is reserved for severe wasting and/or nutritional oedema cases.
Signs of some dehydration include restlessness, sunken eyes, eager drinking, and slow skin pinch.
Severe dehydration is indicated by diarrhea plus lethargy, very slow skin pinch, or shock signs.
Observation or admission is recommended if intake is poor or home care is unreliable.
Emergency stabilization should not be delayed by the malnutrition label.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: For children 6–59 months with moderate wasting (MAM) and dehydration but not shock, the 2023 WHO wasting guideline recommends low-osmolarity ORS in accordance with standard diarrhea recommendations for all children.
